The bull case for NVIDIA needs only one assumption: that hyperscaler capital spending keeps compounding. The bear case is that we are watching the top of an investment cycle, and the warning lights are already blinking.

Start with the lease checks. TD Cowen's supply-chain work found Microsoft walked away from data-center projects totaling roughly two gigawatts of capacity across the U.S. and Europe over six months — driven, in their read, by a decision not to support incremental OpenAI training workloads. Microsoft disputes the interpretation and reiterated its roughly $80 billion spending plan, and Google and Meta appear to have backfilled some of ..."}, {"chunk_id": "08_hyperscaler_capex_commentary_msft_goog_m::000", "title": "Hyperscaler capex commentary (MSFT/GOOG/META Q1 calls)", "published_at": "2025-05-02", "score": 18.064, "excerpt": "# Hyperscaler capex commentary (MSFT/GOOG/META Q1 calls)

The March-quarter earnings round from the big cloud buyers — Alphabet (April 24), Microsoft and Meta (April 30), and Amazon (May 1) — settled, at least for now, the question hanging over AI infrastructure names since the winter's data-center-lease scare: the 2025 budgets are intact, and one of them went up.

Meta raised its full-year 2025 capital-expenditure outlook to $64–72 billion, from $60–65 billion previously, citing additional data-center investments to support AI and higher expected costs of infrastructure hardware. Mark Zuckerberg framed the spend around AI now touching nearly every Meta product.

NVIDIA disclosed $11.0 billion of Blackwell revenue in the January quarter — its fastest product ramp ever — after early GB200 NVL72 rack-integration and thermal issues reported in late 2024 slowed initial system builds. ODM partners and hyperscalers have since moved to volume rack shipments, and TSMC has been expanding CoWoS advanced-packaging capacity through 2025 to relieve the main bottleneck. The open question for the ramp is less demand than mix: rack-scale NVL72 systems carry more content per GPU but are harder to build, test, and deploy than HGX boards, and the GB300 transition later this year adds another changeover.

args.reason: Sufficient evidence gathered: FY26Q1 results (revenue $44.1B beat vs $43.3B consensus; $4.5B H20 charge in Q1; ex-H20 gross margin 71.3% and EPS $0.96), Q2 guidance ($45.0B ±2% with ~$8B H20 revenue loss baked in, GM 72% non-GAAP), Blackwell ramp ($11B in Q4 FY25, fastest product ramp ever, top-4 cloud ordered 3.6M GPUs), hyperscaler capex intact ($300B+ combined 2025, Meta raised to $64-72B), bear case (capex digestion risk in 2026, customer concentration with 10%+ direct customers, China business removed, depreciation treadmill), and price action (stock +3.2% on May 29 to $139.00, up 28.4% over 60 days into the print). Total H20 hit = $12.5B ($4.5B Q1 charge + $8B Q2 revenue loss). All key claims horizon elements covered.
